topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery driver, a young man with a perpetually tired expression, placed the bouquet on the doorstep. They were vibrant sunflowers, impossibly bright against the dull grey of the concrete. My hand, reaching for the box, froze halfway. There was no note, no inscription, nothing to explain the sudden, extravagant gesture. My heart began to thump a frantic rhythm against my ribs, a sudden, tight constriction in my chest. I slowly lowered my hand, my gaze fixed on the stems, wondering what unspoken message lay hidden beneath the petals. I backed away from the door, my breath catching in my throat, the silence of the apartment amplifying the sudden, jarring quiet in my mind.

[story 2]

I found the arrangement sitting on my kitchen counter, nestled beside the morning newspaper. Roses, deep crimson, dripping with dew that hadn't been there moments before. I picked one up, the velvet petals cool against my palm. My fingers traced the edges, searching for any clue, any indication of who might have sent them. My eyes darted around the room, taking in the untouched surfaces, the perfectly ordered bookshelves. A coldness seeped into my limbs, making my skin prickle. Who would do this? The sheer unexpectedness of it sent a tremor through me, and I stood rigid, unable to move toward the door, waiting for something to happen.

[story 3]

The package arrived with a soft thud. Inside, a cascade of lilies, their fragrance thick and overwhelming in the sterile air of my hallway. I picked up the box, the cardboard feeling strangely heavy. As I lifted the lid, the scent hit me—intense, almost suffocating. My jaw tightened involuntarily. I scanned the empty space beside the flowers, expecting a slip of paper, a signature, anything to contextualize this opulent display. The lack of communication felt like a deliberate void, a calculated omission that made the hairs on my arms stand straight up. I took a sharp, shallow breath, my knuckles white where I gripped the box.
